summary:
Vlasac is a Mesolithic settlement in the Iron Gates gorge of the Danube River, located in modern Serbia close to the better-known site of [[event:1276|Lepenski Vir]], with which it shares many cultural characteristics. Occupied from approximately 9500 to 6000 BCE, Vlasac was a semi-sedentary community of hunter-fishers who subsisted primarily on the abundant fish resources of the Danube gorge, supplemented by hunting and gathering in the surrounding upland forests.

Archaeological work at Vlasac has yielded burials, structural remains, and faunal assemblages that closely parallel those from Lepenski Vir, confirming the existence of a culturally coherent Iron Gates Mesolithic tradition along this stretch of the Danube. Skeletal analysis of Vlasac burials has contributed importantly to understanding the genetics, diet, and health of late Mesolithic and early Neolithic populations in southeastern Europe. Like Lepenski Vir, Vlasac shows evidence of contact and eventual replacement by or integration with early Neolithic farming communities of Anatolian origin — a process that genetic studies have shown involved complex patterns of admixture rather than simple displacement.
